summary.executiveSummary: The move_auditor::escrow module implements a single-job payment escrow for audit fees on Sui. Its fundamental security flaw is that the payer can unilaterally call refund() at any time—including after the audit completes—providing no binding payment guarantee to the authority. The status tracking system (STATUS_LOCKED) is entirely dead code: the field is set at creation but never read or validated, so the intended lifecycle is unenforced. Sui's object model prevents double-spend, but the absence of mutual-consent gates, time-locks, or proof-of-delivery checks means this contract cannot be safely used in a trust-minimized payment context.

findings.title: Payer Can Unilaterally Self-Refund — No Binding Payment Guarantee for Authority
findings.description: refund() permits either the payer OR the authority to trigger a refund with no preconditions. Because the payer is an authorized caller, they can invoke refund() at any point in the lifecycle—including immediately after the authority delivers the audit report. There is no time-lock, mutual-agreement gate, proof-of-delivery check, or state transition that prevents this. The escrow is therefore non-binding from the authority's perspective.
findings.impact: A payer can submit refund() the moment the audit report is delivered, retrieving their locked funds without paying the authority. The authority has no on-chain mechanism to prevent this. This renders the escrow ineffective for trust-minimized payment enforcement and enables systematic non-payment.
findings.recommendation: Remove the payer from the authorized callers of refund(). Only the authority should be able to initiate a refund (i.e., voluntarily waive payment). For dispute resolution, implement a multisig refund requiring both payer and authority signatures, or add a trusted arbiter address. A time-lock approach is also viable: after a delivery window expires without capture, a payer-initiated refund becomes callable.
findings.codeSnippet:
assert!(sender == escrow.payer || sender == escrow.authority, ENotAuthorized);
// payer can call refund() at any time — no proof-of-work or time gate

findings.title: Race Condition — Payer Can Front-Run Authority's capture() with refund()
findings.description: Both capture() and refund() are gated only by payer/authority address membership with no ordering constraint or mutual-exclusion mechanism beyond Sui's object consumption. A payer monitoring the network can observe the authority's capture() broadcast and submit a competing refund() in the same or earlier checkpoint. Sui does not guarantee transaction ordering within a checkpoint in favor of either party. Whichever transaction is sequenced first consumes the shared object; the other aborts with no recourse.
findings.impact: A payer acting in bad faith can race to refund after the authority broadcasts capture(), extracting funds and forcing the authority to absorb the full cost of completed audit work. This attack requires no special privilege beyond being the recorded payer and observing network activity.
findings.recommendation: Separate the authority's capture right from the payer's refund right with an explicit time-based or state-based ordering: e.g., only the authority may act during a primary window; the payer may refund only after a mandatory expiry epoch if the authority has not captured. This eliminates the competitive race.

findings.title: STATUS_LOCKED Constant and status Field Are Dead Code — Lifecycle Never Enforced
findings.description: STATUS_LOCKED (value 0) is assigned to escrow.status at creation in lock(), but both capture() and refund() destructure the struct with status: _, discarding the field without reading it. No STATUS_CAPTURED or STATUS_REFUNDED variants exist. The entire status tracking system is inert—there is no state-machine enforcement anywhere in the module.
findings.impact: The dead status field gives a false impression of lifecycle enforcement to readers and integrators. Any off-chain indexer querying escrow.status to determine lifecycle phase will always observe LOCKED until the object is deleted. A future upgrade author who does not notice the field is always 0 may introduce incorrect state checks. The code comment documents a lifecycle that the code does not implement.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) delete the status field and STATUS_LOCKED constant entirely—object deletion via object::delete(id) is the idiomatic Sui terminal state and is sufficient—or (b) define STATUS_CAPTURED and STATUS_REFUNDED variants, assert status == STATUS_LOCKED at the top of both capture() and refund(), and update the field before deletion. Option (a) is simpler and preferred.

findings.title: Authority Can Unilaterally Refund Completed Audits — Compromised Key Drains Revenue
findings.description: The authority is permitted to call refund(), returning funds to the payer. The code comment frames this as a convenience for Ctrl+C rollback, but a compromised, stolen, or buggy authority key can refund any open escrow at will, transferring all locked funds back to payers without payment. There is no minimum-hold period, delivery checkpoint, or confirmation step before the authority can issue a refund.
findings.impact: A compromised authority private key can drain all in-progress and completed escrows to their respective payers, causing total revenue loss for the service operator. Because the authority is a single address (not a multisig), the blast radius is unbounded across all concurrent escrow objects.
findings.recommendation: Document explicitly that the authority key has full refund power and must be protected with HSM or multisig key management. Architecturally, consider restricting authority-initiated refund to a specific time window (e.g., only within N epochs of lock) or requiring a reason code event so operators can audit unilateral refunds. Replacing the single authority address with a multisig is the strongest mitigation.
findings.codeSnippet:
assert!(sender == escrow.payer || sender == escrow.authority, ENotAuthorized);
// authority calling refund() returns payer's money — no guard against post-delivery refund

findings.title: No Validation on authority Address — Zero Address Burns Funds, Self-Authority Bypasses Escrow
findings.description: lock() accepts any address as authority with no validation. Two edge cases: (1) authority == @0x0 — capture() transfers funds to the zero address, which no one controls on Sui, effectively burning them permanently. (2) authority == payer — the payer becomes both parties; they can call both capture() and refund() unilaterally, making the escrow trivially escapable with no counterparty guarantee at all.
findings.impact: User error or a malicious CLI client supplying a bad authority address results in burned funds or a meaningless escrow. Depending on how the CLI constructs the authority address (e.g., from a config file that could be tampered), this could be exploited in a supply-chain attack against the payer.
findings.recommendation: Add to lock(): assert!(authority != @0x0, EInvalidAuthority); assert!(authority != tx_context::sender(ctx), EInvalidAuthority); The EscrowLocked event already emits the authority address, providing a secondary off-chain validation point.

findings.title: Redundant amount Field — Events Emit Stale Lock-Time Value Instead of Actual Balance
findings.description: The amount field stores coin::value(&payment) at lock time. The public amount() helper correctly reads balance::value(&escrow.funds), but EscrowCaptured and EscrowRefunded events emit the stored amount field rather than the live balance at transfer time. If a future upgrade or composable extension modifies funds directly (possible in Sui's composable object model), events will report the original lock amount rather than the actual amount transferred, silently corrupting accounting records.
findings.impact: Low immediate risk under current code. In any future upgrade that adjusts the balance post-lock (e.g., fee deduction, partial release), downstream systems relying on event amounts for financial reconciliation will observe incorrect values with no on-chain indication of the discrepancy.
findings.recommendation: Remove the amount field from the struct. Compute the emission value from balance::value(&funds) at the point of destructuring, before coin::from_balance consumes it. This eliminates the redundancy and guarantees event accuracy regardless of future balance changes.
findings.codeSnippet:
amount: u64,  // frozen at lock() time
...
event::emit(EscrowCaptured { escrow_id, authority, amount });  // stale if balance diverged post-lock

findings.title: Zero-Amount Escrow Creation Allowed — Storage Spam Vector
findings.description: lock() does not assert that the payment amount exceeds zero. A zero-value Coin<T> is valid in Sui, so any caller can create unbounded spam escrow objects with no economic commitment, consuming shared object slots and emitting EscrowLocked events without meaningful payment.
findings.impact: Negligible per-object economic impact. At scale, zero-amount escrows could contribute to Sui state bloat, inflate event indexes, and confuse off-chain monitoring systems that parse EscrowLocked events to track payment volume.
findings.recommendation: Add: assert!(coin::value(&payment) > 0, EZeroAmount); at the top of lock(), with a corresponding error constant const EZeroAmount: u64 = 2;
findings.codeSnippet: let amount = coin::value(&payment);  // no minimum enforcement

expensivePatterns: Shared object model: transfer::share_object() makes AuditEscrow a shared object, requiring Sui consensus for every capture/refund transaction. Owned objects bypass consensus and use the cheaper fast-path. For high-frequency small-value audits this is a significant per-transaction overhead.

object::uid_to_inner(&id) is called after struct destructuring in both capture() and refund() — the UID is accessed after the struct is already being torn down, which is a minor but avoidable sequencing awkwardness.

Three separate event structs (EscrowLocked, EscrowCaptured, EscrowRefunded) with copy+drop, each emitted on state transitions — unavoidable but worth noting if batch audit flows are designed in future.
optimizationSuggestions: Switch from shared object to owned object: transfer the AuditEscrow to the authority address after lock(), so the authority holds it as an owned object. This enables Sui's owned-object fast-path (no consensus required for capture/refund) and significantly reduces per-transaction gas. The tradeoff is the payer loses direct on-chain access to the object, which aligns with removing payer-initiated refund anyway.
Remove the redundant amount field to save 8 bytes of on-chain storage per escrow object.
Remove the dead status field to save 1 byte of on-chain storage per escrow object.

If multiple concurrent audits per payer are common, a registry pattern (single shared BalanceTable keyed by escrow ID) reduces per-audit object creation overhead and consolidates shared object consensus into one object.

overallRecommendations: Remove payer from refund() authorized callers immediately — this is the primary security failure. An escrow where the payer can self-refund at will provides no payment guarantee and can be systematically abused to avoid paying for completed work.

Implement a time-locked state machine: a LOCKED phase where only capture() is callable, followed by an optional DISPUTED/EXPIRED phase where payer-initiated refund is permitted only after a mandatory window (e.g., 7 epochs) with no authority action.
Replace the single authority address with a multisig or program-derived address to eliminate the single-point-of-key-compromise risk for authority-initiated refunds.
Validate authority address in lock(): reject @0x0 and authority == payer to prevent fund burning and trivially bypassable escrow creation.
Delete the status field and STATUS_LOCKED constant entirely — they are dead code creating false lifecycle assurances. Sui object deletion is the canonical terminal state.
Replace the stored amount field with live balance::value(&funds) reads at capture/refund time to guarantee event accuracy and eliminate the redundant storage slot.
Consider switching to an owned-object model (transfer to authority at lock time) to eliminate shared-object consensus overhead and enable Sui fast-path execution for all escrow operations.
Add assert!(coin::value(&payment) > 0, EZeroAmount) in lock() to prevent zero-value spam escrow creation.
